Assesses duration and frequency to support insomnia diagnosis (e.g., symptoms at least 3 nights/week and for at least 3 months for chronic insomnia) and, if shorter, characterizes as short-term insomnia while still explaining insomnia as the working diagnosis.

Screens for common contributors and secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, medications/substances, pain, nocturia, GERD, anxiety/depression, restless legs symptoms, and obstructive sleep apnea red flags (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness).
Identifies and addresses immediate safety risks (suicidal ideation, severe depression/mania, dangerous daytime sleepiness affecting driving/operating machinery) and escalates care if present.
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and provides a brief, patient-friendly rationale linking symptoms and impairment.

Communication milestone: explains treatment emphasizing first-line CBT-I and uses the terms "insomnia" and "CBT-I" explicitly; includes at least one CBT-I component (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive therapy, relaxation, sleep hygiene).

Avoids prescribing or recommending sedative-hypnotics as the primary first-line approach in a basic L1 diagnosis task; if medications are discussed, frames them as adjunct/short-term only after CBT-I considerations and safety screening.

Performs required safety checks BEFORE any prescribing is attempted: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), checks for contraindications (contraindication_check, e.g., pregnancy, severe respiratory disease/OSA risk, substance use disorder history, older adult fall risk), and checks for drug interactions (drug_interaction_check); if not prescribing, does not need to force these checks.
Provides a reasonable follow-up timeframe and monitoring plan in narrative form (even without using a follow-up tool), including tracking sleep with a sleep diary and reassessing response to CBT-I.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia after core history and exclusion of key secondary causes; explain diagnosis and first-line CBT-I plan; no medications prescribed.
must_collect: sleep onset latency and/or nighttime awakenings
screen for OSA symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, daytime sleepiness)
current medications that may affect sleep
steps: Take targeted insomnia history (pattern, duration/frequency, impairment) and assess sleep environment/behaviors.
Screen for common secondary causes and red flags (OSA, RLS, mood disorder/mania, substance use, safety risks).
Explain the diagnosis using the word "insomnia" and provide rationale.
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I for insomnia and outline key components; recommend sleep diary.
Document diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Classifies as short-term vs chronic insomnia based on duration while still documenting insomnia as the diagnosis; provides CBT-I plan and follow-up advice.
steps: Assess insomnia symptoms, duration/frequency, and impairment; determine short-term vs chronic features.
Screen for OSA/RLS, mood/anxiety, substances/medications, and safety risks.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and expected course.
Recommend CBT-I and sleep diary; discuss sleep hygiene as supportive.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Discusses medication options only at a high level (without prescribing) and emphasizes that CBT-I is first-line; includes safety caveats about sedatives and driving/alcohol.
steps: Elicit core insomnia history and daytime impact; review substances/meds and screen for OSA/RLS and mood disorder.
Explain insomnia diagnosis.
Explain CBT-I as first-line for insomnia; add brief mention that medications may be considered short-term in select cases after safety screening.
Provide safety counseling (avoid alcohol with sedatives, caution with driving if sleepy).
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

nl_assertions: States a clear leading diagnosis of acute appendicitis and communicates uncertainty appropriately if data are incomplete.

Prednisone 20 mg PO daily
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS 1 tablet PO three times weekly
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily

confounders: Chronic corticosteroid therapy for organizing pneumonia (immunosuppression; blunts fever and inflammatory signs)
Baseline dyspnea and chronic inflammatory lung disease can mimic or mask acute infectious pneumonia
Prior antibiotic exposure (ceftriaxone) may partially treat typical CAP pathogens and alter culture yield, delaying recognition of staphylococcal pneumonia
Possible aspiration risk due to age and intermittent reflux (can present with similar consolidation and leukocytosis)

required_evidence: Elevated WBC with neutrophilia and bandemia

medications: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h

non_pharmacological: IV fluids if hypotensive or elevated lactate

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 24-48 hours; outpatient follow-up 1-2 weeks after discharge

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirms the correct clinical working diagnosis to anchor appropriate empiric/targeted therapy and monitoring.
Initiates appropriate first-line IV anti-MRSA coverage when staphylococcal pneumonia is suspected/diagnosed, reducing morbidity and mortality.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'staphylococcal pneumonia' and explains what it means in patient-friendly language.
Explains treatment plan including the need for 'IV antibiotics' and specifically names 'vancomycin' as part of therapy.
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any antibiotic; if a relevant allergy is present, selects a safe alternative and documents rationale.
Performs a contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., evaluates renal function/AKI risk and appropriateness of vancomycin dosing/monitoring); adjusts plan if contraindications exist.
Checks for clinically important drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical); if interactions are identified, mitigates risk (dose adjustment, monitoring, or alternative).
Demonstrates appropriate severity assessment and disposition awareness (e.g., recognizes need for inpatient/IV therapy and monitoring when indicated) without delaying antibiotics in severe illness.
Provides safety-net instructions: worsening dyspnea, persistent fever, confusion, cyanosis, chest pain, or inability to maintain hydration should prompt urgent reassessment.

Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., oral-only outpatient therapy for clearly severe disease, or using beta-lactams alone when MRSA is a concern) and avoids contraindicated antibiotics when risk factors exist.

minimal_paths.description: Record staphylococcal pneumonia and start empiric IV anti-MRSA therapy with vancomycin after required safety checks.
must_collect: renal disease or kidney function status
severity indicators (hypoxia, respiratory distress, hypotension, altered mental status)
steps: Explain working diagnosis: staphylococcal pneumonia.
Confirm allergies prior to prescribing.
Confirm contraindications/renal status and plan for dosing/monitoring as appropriate.
Check for major drug interactions (non-critical) and mitigate if present.
Prescribe IV antibiotics including vancomycin.
Explain expected course and monitoring needs; provide return precautions.

acceptable_variants.description: If severe pneumonia or high concern for polymicrobial infection, add concurrent broad Gram-negative coverage while still including vancomycin for MRSA.
steps: Record diagnosis: staphylococcal pneumonia.
Allergy and contraindication checks.
Prescribe vancomycin IV plus an appropriate additional IV agent for broader coverage based on local patterns and patient risk factors.
Explain IV antibiotic plan including vancomycin and why combination therapy is used.
Provide monitoring and safety-net guidance.

acceptable_variants.description: If vancomycin is contraindicated (e.g., severe intolerance or other compelling contraindication), use an appropriate IV anti-MRSA alternative and document why, while still meeting communication milestones by discussing vancomycin as standard therapy.
steps: Record diagnosis: staphylococcal pneumonia.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks identifying vancomycin unsuitability.
Prescribe an appropriate IV anti-MRSA alternative (e.g., linezolid IV) with rationale and monitoring plan.
Explain treatment: IV antibiotics are needed; vancomycin is standard but an alternative is chosen due to patient-specific contraindication.
Provide follow-up/return precautions and monitoring plan.
